title: Candle Wax on Cotton
stainChemistry: Candle wax (paraffin) is a solid petroleum hydrocarbon that solidifies on cotton as it cools, trapping any dye from colored candles within the wax matrix. The wax itself is not a stain — it's a physical deposit. However, dyed candles leave pigment behind after wax removal. The wax must be removed before the dye can be treated.
whyThisWorks: Freezing makes wax brittle on cotton, allowing mechanical removal of the bulk deposit. Low heat (iron through paper towel) melts remaining wax into the absorbent paper by capillary action. POG dissolves any wax residue. If dye remains after wax removal, it's treated as a separate dye stain with appropriate chemistry.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Freeze the wax with ice bag to harden it. Scrape off bulk with dull edge
2. Apply POG to dissolve remaining wax residue
3. Apply NSD to lift pigment (for colored wax). Flush with cool water
4. If dye remains: apply H2O2 6% carefully, monitor
5. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) for pH restoration. Blot dry
warnings: Apply solvent BEFORE water — water spreads oil stains

homeSolutions.agent: Two-stage plan: physical first, chemistry second
homeSolutions.instruction: Candle wax is unusual — the wax itself isn't a stain, it's a physical deposit. The real stain (if any) is pigment from a colored candle hiding inside that wax. Your job: get 95% of the wax off MECHANICALLY (freeze + scrape + iron), then treat any remaining oil or dye with normal chemistry. Do NOT skip to washing — laundry detergent + wax = wax smeared across the whole load of clothes.

homeSolutions.agent: Freeze the wax brittle — ice in a bag, not on fabric
homeSolutions.instruction: Place a ziplock bag of ice cubes (or a frozen peas bag) directly on top of the wax. Leave 10–15 minutes. You want the wax rock-hard and shattery. Never apply ice DIRECTLY to cotton — condensation spreads stains. If the item fits, you can put it in the freezer in a ziplock for 30 minutes instead.

homeSolutions.agent: Scrape + flake off with a dull edge
homeSolutions.instruction: Once the wax is hard, crack it gently and lift off large chunks with a butter knife or the edge of a credit card. Work from the outside in. Brittle wax pops off in satisfying chunks — if it's still soft or smearing, re-freeze. Bend the fabric over on itself to pop remaining wax out of the weave. Don't use a sharp knife — it cuts the fabric.

homeSolutions.agent: Iron melt-through — sandwich between paper towels
homeSolutions.instruction: Place a piece of brown paper bag or four layers of clean white paper towel UNDER the wax spot and another four layers on TOP. Set your iron to LOW heat (wool/silk setting — never cotton/linen high). Press down for 10 seconds, lift, move to a clean section of the paper, press again. The remaining wax melts UP and DOWN into the paper and out of the fiber. Replace the paper every time you see it absorb wax. Keep going until the paper stops picking up anything.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Test your iron temperature on a corner of the paper first — too hot and the paper browns or scorches the fabric. Never use steam here. Never press wax directly (without paper) — you melt it INTO the fabric permanently.

homeSolutions.instruction: Before applying any solvent or Dawn, dab a drop on an inside seam and blot after 60 seconds. If the fabric holds its color, proceed. If dye transfers, go to step 9 — home solvents will strip your cotton dye.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + dry-side work for residual wax oil
homeSolutions.instruction: Even after the iron step there's often an oil film from the paraffin left behind. Put a small drop of clear Dawn directly on the spot (no water yet), work it in with a fingertip for 30 seconds, let sit 10 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Dawn is the best consumer-grade degreaser for paraffin residue. Blot from the back of the fabric if possible.

homeSolutions.agent: Treat any remaining dye (colored candles only)
homeSolutions.instruction: If the candle was white, you're done — skip to step 8. If the candle was colored (red, blue, green, etc.), you may see pigment left behind after the wax is gone. That's now a DYE stain — treat with rubbing alcohol: dab isopropyl alcohol 91% on a clean white cloth and press onto the pigment, replacing the pad underneath as it picks up dye. Work outside in. If color won't budge, skip to step 9.

Launder on warm (not hot) with your normal detergent. Air-dry and inspect in daylight. Any greasy shadow means residual paraffin — repeat step 6 before using the dryer. Dryer heat on residual wax melts it back into surrounding fabric and can transfer wax to other items.

If colored-candle dye won't lift, the wax was already through a hot dryer, or significant residue survives two home rounds, take it in. Tell them: "Candle wax on cotton, [plain/colored]. Removed bulk mechanically (freeze + iron-through-paper), treated residue with Dawn + [alcohol]. Please use POG solvent for paraffin film and dye-side treatment for residual pigment." That specifies exactly what they need to do.

steps: 1. Freeze the wax: place ice in a bag on the wax for 10 minutes until brittle. Do NOT apply ice directly to fabric
2. Scrape off hardened wax with a dull knife or credit card edge
3. Place paper towels above and below the fabric (if possible). Iron on low heat to melt remaining wax into paper — check every 5 seconds
4. Apply Dawn dish soap to any remaining residue. Blot
5. Rinse with warm water. Air dry — inspect before using dryer
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 treatment rounds. Take to a professional dry cleaner — they have specialized solvents for oil-based stains
warnings: Never add water to a fresh oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, you will spread it
Heat from dryer permanently sets oil residue

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
fiberSensitivities: Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide

escalation.when: If candle wax stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is candle wax on cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the oil fresh or has it been washed/dried already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
